title: Design tables around the clustered primary key
description: Pick a small, monotonic primary key because InnoDB physically orders every row and every secondary index by it.
body: Use when choosing a primary key for a new InnoDB table or reviewing one with a wide or random key. Do not use a random UUIDv4 or a wide composite as the clustered key on a write-heavy table; every secondary index stores the full PK, so a fat key bloats all of them and random inserts fragment the B-tree. Actions: prefer a BIGINT auto-increment or a time-ordered UUIDv7, verify with SHOW TABLE STATUS that index size is proportional to rows, and run a bulk-insert benchmark comparing page splits. Validation signals include a flat insert-latency curve and secondary indexes staying compact. Risk boundary: changing the PK of a populated table rewrites the whole clustered index, so escalate it to a windowed migration.
